name: Alzheimer's Disease
description: Progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by amyloid-beta plaques, neurofibrillary tau tangles, synaptic loss, and widespread cortical neuronal death leading to dementia. The most common cause of dementia, with incidence doubling every five years after age 65.

mechanism_links.notes: Aβ plaques are pathognomonic; amyloid cascade hypothesis central
Neurofibrillary tangles correlate with disease stage
Synaptic loss best correlates with cognitive decline

name: Parkinson's Disease
description: Neurodegenerative disorder defined by loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and presence of Lewy body inclusions containing alpha-synuclein. Cardinal features include resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability.

mechanism_links.notes: Lewy bodies are pathological hallmark; αSyn propagates prion-like
PINK1-Parkin pathway loss-of-function causes familial PD
Dopamine metabolism generates oxidative burden in SNpc

name: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
description: Fatal neurodegenerative disease of upper and lower motor neurons leading to progressive paralysis and respiratory failure. TDP-43 proteinopathy is present in >97% of cases; SOD1, FUS, and C9orf72 mutations account for most familial cases.

mechanism_links.notes: TDP-43, FUS, SOD1 aggregation in motor neurons
Defective retrograde transport precedes motor neuron death
Mitochondrial clustering and dysfunction in motor axons
Activated microglia and astrogliosis accelerate disease

name: Vascular Dementia
description: Second most common cause of dementia, resulting from cumulative cerebrovascular injury including large vessel strokes, small vessel disease, white matter hyperintensities, and lacunar infarcts. Often co-occurs with Alzheimer's pathology.

mechanism_links.notes: Endothelial dysfunction and BBB breakdown central
Atherosclerotic risk factors drive large vessel disease
Ischemia-reperfusion ROS damage to brain tissue
White matter lesions disrupt cortico-subcortical circuits

name: Lewy Body Dementia
description: Second most common neurodegenerative dementia, characterized by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, and REM sleep behavior disorder. Cortical and limbic Lewy bodies containing alpha-synuclein are the pathological substrate.

mechanism_links.notes: Cortical and limbic Lewy body deposition drives neuronal dysfunction
Cholinergic and dopaminergic synaptic loss underlies symptoms

name: Huntington's Disease
description: Autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by CAG repeat expansion in the HTT gene, producing mutant huntingtin protein that aggregates in striatal and cortical neurons. Manifests with chorea, cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms in midlife.

mechanism_links.notes: Mutant huntingtin aggregates sequester functional proteins
mHTT loads autophagosome but impairs cargo recognition
PARP hyperactivation by DNA damage depletes NAD+

name: Multiple Sclerosis
description: Chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of the CNS with progressive neurological disability. Autoreactive T and B cells attack myelin sheaths; with aging the progressive phase dominates due to neurodegeneration and failed remyelination rather than acute inflammation.

mechanism_links.notes: Exhausted regulatory T cells fail to suppress autoreactivity
Oxidative damage to exposed axons in chronic lesions

name: Late-Onset Epilepsy
description: New-onset seizures after age 60, frequently caused by cortical scarring from stroke, neurodegeneration, or tumors. Aging-associated changes in neuronal excitability, GABAergic inhibition decline, and increased cortical hyperexcitability contribute to seizure threshold lowering.

mechanism_links.notes: Cerebrovascular disease is the leading structural cause

name: Atherosclerosis
description: Chronic inflammatory disease of large and medium arteries characterized by lipid-laden plaque formation in the intima. Foam cell accumulation, fibrous cap formation, and plaque rupture cause acute coronary syndromes and stroke. Accelerated by age-related endothelial dysfunction and systemic inflammation.

mechanism_links.notes: Endothelial dysfunction and monocyte recruitment initiate plaque
oxLDL uptake by macrophages forms foam cells
Senescent vascular smooth muscle cells destabilize plaque
ROS oxidize LDL and impair endothelial NO synthesis

name: Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF)
description: Heart failure syndrome with normal or near-normal left ventricular ejection fraction but impaired diastolic filling due to myocardial stiffness. Driven by systemic inflammation, metabolic syndrome, and cardiac fibrosis. Predominantly affects older obese women and lacks approved disease-modifying therapies.

mechanism_links.notes: Collagen crosslinking and TGF-β fibrosis reduce LV compliance
Metabolic syndrome is dominant comorbidity; hyperinsulinemia drives cardiac hypertrophy
Senescent cardiomyocytes contribute to diastolic dysfunction

name: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction (HFrEF)
description: Heart failure characterized by reduced left ventricular systolic function (EF <40%), typically following myocardial infarction or dilated cardiomyopathy. Cardiomyocyte loss, maladaptive remodeling, and neurohormonal activation drive progressive contractile failure.

mechanism_links.notes: Mitochondrial dysfunction central to cardiomyocyte energy failure
Senescent cardiomyocytes and fibroblasts impair contractile function
Ischemia-reperfusion ROS damage and ongoing oxidative stress

name: Atrial Fibrillation
description: Most common cardiac arrhythmia, with prevalence rising steeply with age. Atrial fibrosis, electrical remodeling, and autonomic dysregulation create the substrate for chaotic atrial depolarization. Dramatically increases stroke risk and heart failure progression.

mechanism_links.notes: Atrial fibrosis creates reentrant arrhythmia substrate
Inflammatory state promotes atrial structural remodeling
ROS impair calcium handling in atrial cardiomyocytes
Senescent atrial fibroblasts drive fibrotic remodeling

name: Hypertension
description: Sustained elevation of arterial blood pressure, the most prevalent age-related cardiovascular risk factor. Driven by arterial stiffening, impaired endothelial function, sympathetic activation, and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system dysregulation. Leads to target organ damage in heart, brain, and kidneys.

mechanism_links.notes: Arterial stiffening due to elastin degradation and collagen crosslinking increases pulse pressure
ROS scavenge NO and activate vasoconstrictive signaling
Hyperinsulinemia promotes sodium retention and sympathetic activation

name: Coronary Artery Disease
description: Atherosclerotic narrowing of epicardial coronary arteries causing myocardial ischemia. Age is the dominant non-modifiable risk factor. Plaque rupture causes acute MI; stable angina from demand-supply mismatch in chronic disease.

mechanism_links.notes: LDL accumulation and oxidation drive plaque formation
Endothelial inflammation recruits monocytes to intima
TET2-mutant macrophages produce excess IL-1β in plaques
Oxidative modification of LDL and endothelial cells

name: Aortic Stenosis
description: Progressive calcification and fibrosis of the aortic valve leaflets leading to obstruction of left ventricular outflow. Most common valvular heart disease in the elderly; shares pathobiological features with atherosclerosis. Treated by surgical or transcatheter valve replacement.

mechanism_links.notes: Fibro-calcific remodeling of valve leaflets by myofibroblasts
Lipid deposition and oxLDL in valve initiates disease
Oxidative stress activates valve interstitial cells

name: Cardiac Hypertrophy
description: Pathological enlargement of the myocardium in response to chronic pressure or volume overload, neurohormonal activation, and direct aging effects. Maladaptive hypertrophy leads to fibrosis, diastolic dysfunction, and eventual heart failure.

mechanism_links.notes: mTORC1 promotes cardiomyocyte protein synthesis and hypertrophy
Interstitial fibrosis accompanies and perpetuates hypertrophy
Impaired mitophagy increases oxidative burden in hypertrophied cardiomyocytes
NF-κB drives pro-hypertrophic and pro-fibrotic gene programs

name: Type 2 Diabetes
description: Metabolic disorder characterized by progressive insulin resistance, compensatory hyperinsulinemia, and eventual pancreatic beta-cell failure leading to hyperglycemia. Strongly age-associated; complications affect cardiovascular, renal, neurological, and ophthalmological systems.

mechanism_links.notes: Peripheral insulin resistance is the primary defect in T2D
Beta-cell ER stress drives apoptosis under chronic glucose/lipid load
IAPP amyloid deposits destroy islet beta-cells
Visceral adiposity and lipotoxicity drive insulin resistance

pubmed_search_terms: T2D aging insulin resistance beta cell

name: Metabolic Syndrome
description: Cluster of interrelated metabolic abnormalities including central obesity, hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL, hypertension, and hyperglycemia. Substantially increases risk for T2D and cardiovascular disease. Prevalence rises sharply with age.

mechanism_links.notes: Visceral adipose dysfunction is central to metabolic syndrome
Insulin resistance links all components of the syndrome
Dyslipidemia driven by ectopic lipid and impaired clearance
Gut-derived LPS activates macrophages promoting metabolic inflammation

name: NAFLD/NASH
description: Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and its inflammatory form NASH represent a spectrum from hepatic steatosis to steatohepatitis, fibrosis, and cirrhosis. Increasingly common with aging, obesity, and metabolic syndrome; a leading cause of hepatocellular carcinoma.

mechanism_links.notes: Increased free fatty acid flux from dysfunctional adipose to liver
Hepatocyte ER stress triggers lipoapoptosis and inflammation
Portal delivery of bacterial products activates hepatic TLR4
Impaired hepatic mitophagy amplifies oxidative stress

pubmed_search_terms: nonalcoholic fatty liver aging inflammation aging

name: Obesity
description: Excess body fat accumulation, particularly visceral adiposity, that promotes systemic inflammation, insulin resistance, and accelerated biological aging. Age-related changes in appetite regulation, energy expenditure, and adipose biology promote weight gain.

mechanism_links.notes: Adipose senescence and inflammation are central to obesity pathology
Adipokine imbalance and lipotoxicity drive peripheral insulin resistance
Obesity-associated dysbiosis increases gut permeability and metabolic endotoxemia

name: Gout
description: Crystal arthropathy caused by monosodium urate deposition in joints, driven by hyperuricemia. Incidence rises steeply with age due to declining renal urate clearance, diuretic use, and metabolic syndrome. NLRP3 inflammasome activation by urate crystals drives acute attacks.

mechanism_links.notes: NF-κB drives IL-1β production in response to urate crystals
Declining GFR reduces renal uric acid excretion
Insulin inhibits renal urate excretion; metabolic syndrome major risk factor
Xanthine oxidase-generated ROS contribute to uric acid production

name: Hypothyroidism
description: Deficient thyroid hormone production causing metabolic slowing, fatigue, cognitive impairment, and cardiovascular risk. Prevalence increases with age; subclinical hypothyroidism is common. Autoimmune destruction (Hashimoto's) and iodine deficiency are leading causes.

mechanism_links.notes: Thyroid hormone is a key metabolic regulator declining with age in some
Thyroid hormone drives PGC-1α and mitochondrial biogenesis
Autoimmune thyroiditis linked to immune dysregulation with aging
Inflammatory signaling impairs thyroid gland function

name: Multiple Myeloma
description: Malignancy of plasma cells in the bone marrow producing monoclonal immunoglobulin. Median age at diagnosis is 69. Bone marrow microenvironment aging, genomic instability, and immunosenescence contribute to disease initiation and progression from MGUS.

mechanism_links.notes: Clonal B-lineage expansion preceding myeloma shares mechanisms with CHIP
Impaired immune surveillance allows MGUS-to-myeloma progression
Epigenetic drift in plasma cell precursors enables malignant transformation
Aged bone marrow niche supports myeloma cell survival

name: Colorectal Cancer
description: Adenocarcinoma arising from colorectal epithelium, predominantly through chromosomal instability or microsatellite instability pathways. Incidence strongly age-related; cumulative DNA damage, dysbiosis, and impaired immune surveillance drive malignant transformation of adenomatous polyps.

mechanism_links.notes: Promoter hypermethylation silences tumor suppressors (MLH1, CDKN2A)
Bacterial genotoxins (colibactin, CDT) damage colonocyte DNA
NF-κB drives survival, proliferation, and inflammation in tumors

pubmed_search_terms: colon cancer aging DNA damage dysbiosis

name: Lung Cancer
description: Leading cause of cancer mortality, predominantly non-small cell (NSCLC: adenocarcinoma, squamous cell) driven by tobacco carcinogen-induced DNA mutations. Age-related accumulation of somatic mutations and immunosenescence create permissive conditions for malignant transformation.

mechanism_links.notes: Accelerated epigenetic aging in lung epithelium predicts cancer risk
Tobacco-induced and endogenous ROS drive DNA mutation burden
SASP from senescent alveolar cells supports tumor microenvironment

name: Breast Cancer
description: Most common cancer in women, with incidence rising with age. Hormonal changes at menopause, accumulated DNA damage in ductal epithelium, and altered stromal microenvironment all contribute. ER+ subtype predominates in postmenopausal women.

mechanism_links.notes: Estrogen decline at menopause alters breast epithelial biology
BRCA1/2 promoter methylation; hormone receptor gene silencing
Senescent stromal cells promote tumor initiation and progression
Breast adipose inflammation post-menopause promotes tumor growth

name: Prostate Cancer
description: Most common cancer in men, with incidence and lethal potential rising sharply with age. Age-related androgen signaling changes, accumulated somatic mutations, and senescent stromal cells drive malignant transformation of luminal epithelial cells.

mechanism_links.notes: Senescent prostate stromal cells secrete growth factors promoting tumor
Androgen axis changes with aging alter prostate epithelial homeostasis
Epigenetic age acceleration in prostate tissue predicts cancer risk
GSTP1 promoter methylation is hallmark of prostate cancer

name: Pancreatic Cancer
description: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is among the most lethal malignancies with five-year survival under 12%. Age-related KRAS mutations, chronic pancreatitis, and a highly immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment drive rapid progression.

mechanism_links.notes: SASP factors create highly immunosuppressive and desmoplastic stroma
New-onset diabetes often precedes diagnosis; metabolic syndrome is risk factor
NF-κB constitutively active in majority of PDACs
Microbiome alterations contribute to pancreatic cancer pathogenesis

name: Acute Myeloid Leukemia
description: Aggressive hematological malignancy arising from myeloid progenitor cells. Incidence increases markedly with age, frequently arising from clonal hematopoiesis. Associated with poor outcomes in the elderly due to comorbidities, frailty, and adverse cytogenetics.

mechanism_links.notes: CHIP is direct precursor state; DNMT3A/TET2/ASXL1 mutations
TET2 and DNMT3A mutations cause epigenetic dysregulation in HSCs
Aged bone marrow niche favors malignant over normal hematopoiesis
Reduced immunosurveillance allows leukemic clone expansion

pubmed_search_terms: acute myeloid leukemia aging clonal hematopoiesis

name: Melanoma
description: Malignancy of melanocytes driven by UV-induced DNA damage and somatic mutations (BRAF, NRAS). Incidence rises with cumulative UV exposure and aging. Immunosenescence impairs tumor immunosurveillance, allowing emergence of melanocytic malignancy.

mechanism_links.notes: UV-generated ROS drive melanocyte DNA mutation
Age-related T cell exhaustion reduces melanoma immunosurveillance
UV accelerates epigenetic aging in skin
Senescent dermal fibroblasts create pro-tumorigenic niche

pubmed_search_terms: melanoma aging UV DNA damage mechanisms

name: Bladder Cancer
description: Urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with strong age-dependence; median age at diagnosis is 73. Cumulative exposure to carcinogens (tobacco, aromatic amines) and chronic inflammation from recurrent infections drive mucosal DNA damage and malignant transformation.

mechanism_links.notes: Promoter methylation silences tumor suppressors in urothelium
Chronic inflammation activates NF-κB in urothelial cells
Reduced immune elimination of transformed urothelial cells

pubmed_search_terms: bladder cancer aging mechanisms DNA damage

name: Sarcopenia
description: Progressive age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass, strength, and function. Affects >50% of adults over 80 and is a primary driver of frailty, disability, falls, and metabolic dysfunction. Interplay between anabolic resistance, neuromuscular denervation, and inflammation drives muscle wasting.

mechanism_links.notes: Sarcopenia pathway mechanisms directly underlie this condition
Declining mitochondrial biogenesis reduces muscle oxidative capacity
Satellite cell niche dysfunction impairs muscle regeneration

pubmed_search_terms: muscle wasting aging satellite cell mitochondria

name: Osteoporosis
description: Systemic skeletal disease characterized by reduced bone mineral density and microarchitectural deterioration, predisposing to fragility fractures. Major public health problem in aging populations; hip fractures carry high morbidity and mortality.

mechanism_links.notes: Uncoupled bone remodeling favoring resorption is the primary mechanism
Estrogen and testosterone deficiency accelerate osteoclast activity
ROS impair osteoblast differentiation and survival
Senescent osteocytes produce SASP that activates osteoclasts

pubmed_search_terms: bone loss aging osteoblast osteoclast aging

name: Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease characterized by articular cartilage loss, subchondral bone remodeling, osteophyte formation, and synovial inflammation. Most prevalent joint disorder in the elderly; strongly driven by cellular senescence in chondrocytes and synoviocytes.

mechanism_links.notes: MMP-mediated cartilage matrix degradation exceeds synthesis
Senescent chondrocyte SASP drives catabolic cartilage destruction
Subchondral bone stiffening alters cartilage biomechanical load
Mitochondrial ROS in chondrocytes impair matrix synthesis

name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
description: Chronic autoimmune inflammatory arthritis targeting synovial joints with systemic manifestations. Late-onset RA (>60 years) is common and often seronegative; immunosenescence, epigenetic alterations in T cells, and synoviocyte senescence contribute to joint destruction.

mechanism_links.notes: Senescent T cells escape regulation and accumulate in synovium
NF-κB drives synoviocyte invasiveness and cytokine production
MMP-mediated destruction of cartilage and bone

name: Frailty Syndrome
description: Geriatric syndrome of decreased physiological reserve and increased vulnerability to stressors, characterized by unintentional weight loss, exhaustion, weakness, slowness, and low activity. Represents a convergence of multiple aging hallmarks and is the most powerful predictor of adverse outcomes in older adults.

mechanism_links.notes: Sarcopenia is the dominant physical substrate of frailty
Elevated SASP factors drive systemic inflammation in frail individuals
NAD+ deficit impairs mitochondrial function in multiple organ systems
Multi-hormonal deficiency (GH, sex steroids, DHEA) underlies frailty

pubmed_search_terms: frailty syndrome aging SASP inflammation sarcopenia

name: Chronic Kidney Disease
description: Progressive and irreversible loss of kidney function with GFR <60 mL/min for >3 months. Age is a dominant risk factor; CKD accelerates systemic aging through uremic toxin accumulation, inflammation, and mineral-bone disorder.

mechanism_links.notes: Declining GFR through glomerulosclerosis and interstitial fibrosis
TGF-β driven renal interstitial fibrosis replaces functional nephrons
Uremic toxins and reduced antioxidant defense in CKD
Senescent tubular cells perpetuate fibrotic signaling

pubmed_search_terms: chronic kidney disease aging mechanisms hallmarks

name: Diabetic Nephropathy
description: Leading cause of end-stage renal disease, caused by glomerular and tubular damage from hyperglycemia, AGE accumulation, and oxidative stress. Characterized by albuminuria, progressive GFR decline, and glomerular fibrosis.

mechanism_links.notes: Glomerular hypertension and mesangial expansion reduce functional nephron mass
Glucose-driven mitochondrial ROS damage glomerular and tubular cells
AGE crosslinking and TGF-β fibrosis define diabetic glomerulopathy
Hyperglycemia from insulin resistance is the primary driver

pubmed_search_terms: diabetic nephropathy aging oxidative stress mechanisms

name: Renal Fibrosis
description: Final common pathway of virtually all progressive kidney diseases, characterized by tubular atrophy, glomerulosclerosis, and interstitial fibrosis driven by myofibroblast activation and ECM deposition. Irreversible loss of functional kidney architecture.

mechanism_links.notes: TGF-β1 drives pericyte-to-myofibroblast transition and fibrosis
Senescent tubular cells secrete TGF-β and other pro-fibrotic SASP factors
Fibrosis reduces nephron number perpetuating GFR decline
Tubular mitochondrial dysfunction activates fibrotic pathways

name: COPD
description: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease encompasses emphysema and chronic bronchitis, causing irreversible airflow limitation. Tobacco smoke-accelerated lung aging, with cellular senescence, elastin destruction, and impaired repair, are central mechanisms. Leading cause of respiratory morbidity.

mechanism_links.notes: Senescent alveolar epithelial cells and fibroblasts drive emphysematous destruction
MMP-elastase imbalance destroys alveolar walls in emphysema
Impaired autophagy promotes senescence and inflammation in alveolar cells

name: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis
description: Progressive fibrotic lung disease of unknown cause predominantly affecting adults over 60. Accumulation of senescent alveolar type II epithelial cells, failure of regeneration, and aberrant fibroblast activation replace functional lung with scar tissue.

mechanism_links.notes: TGF-β-driven fibroblast activation and collagen deposition
Senescent AEC2 cells drive fibrotic signaling via SASP
Telomere mutations account for ~25% of familial IPF
AEC2 ER stress from misfolded surfactant proteins drives apoptosis

pubmed_search_terms: pulmonary fibrosis aging hallmarks cellular senescence

name: Pulmonary Hypertension
description: Elevated pulmonary arterial pressure from remodeling of small pulmonary arteries, leading to right heart failure. Age-related endothelial dysfunction, inflammation, and vascular smooth muscle cell senescence contribute to pulmonary vascular remodeling.

mechanism_links.notes: Endothelial dysfunction and inflammation drive pulmonary arterial remodeling
Senescent pulmonary arterial endothelial cells impair vascular homeostasis
Mitochondrial dysfunction in pulmonary arterial smooth muscle cells

name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
description: Repetitive upper airway obstruction during sleep causing intermittent hypoxia and sleep fragmentation. Prevalence increases with age and obesity. Intermittent hypoxia drives oxidative stress, systemic inflammation, and accelerates cardiovascular and metabolic aging.

mechanism_links.notes: Intermittent hypoxia-reoxygenation cycles generate burst ROS
Hypoxia activates NF-κB promoting systemic inflammatory cytokines
Visceral obesity is the primary structural risk factor
Intermittent hypoxia impairs mitochondrial quality control

pubmed_search_terms: sleep apnea aging oxidative stress inflammation
OSA aging mechanisms hallmarks intermittent hypoxia

name: Age-Related Macular Degeneration
description: Leading cause of blindness in the elderly, caused by degeneration of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and photoreceptors in the macula. Drusen accumulation, complement dysregulation, and neovascularization (wet AMD) drive progressive central vision loss.

mechanism_links.notes: RPE lysosomal dysfunction leads to lipofuscin and drusen accumulation
High oxygen consumption and light exposure make retina vulnerable to ROS
Senescent RPE cells produce pro-inflammatory SASP factors
Mitochondrial dysfunction in RPE cells impairs phagocytic capacity

name: Glaucoma
description: Progressive optic neuropathy with retinal ganglion cell loss, associated with elevated intraocular pressure. The second leading cause of blindness worldwide; age is the dominant risk factor. Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress in RGCs and trabecular meshwork cells contribute to neurodegeneration.

mechanism_links.notes: ROS impair trabecular meshwork cell function and RGC survival
Mitochondrial dysfunction in RGCs impairs axonal transport and survival
Senescent trabecular meshwork cells reduce aqueous humor outflow
Pressure-induced axonal transport failure at lamina cribrosa

name: Cataracts
description: Age-related opacification of the crystalline lens due to progressive misfolding and aggregation of crystallin proteins. Lens epithelial cells are among the longest-lived non-dividing cells; their lifetime accumulation of oxidative damage and protein aggregation makes cataract a canonical proteostasis disease.

mechanism_links.notes: Crystallin protein aggregation and cross-linking causes lens opacity
Lifetime UV and ROS exposure damages lens crystallins
Impaired autophagy in lens epithelial cells fails to clear damaged proteins
Misfolded crystallins activate UPR in lens cells

name: Diabetic Retinopathy
description: Microvascular complication of diabetes causing pericyte loss, retinal ischemia, neovascularization, and vision loss. Hyperglycemia-driven oxidative stress, inflammation, and AGE accumulation damage retinal vasculature. Leading cause of blindness in working-age adults.

mechanism_links.notes: Glucose-derived ROS damage retinal pericytes and endothelial cells
Endothelial dysfunction and leukostasis in retinal microvasculature
Hyperglycemia activates PKC and AGE pathways in retinal cells

pubmed_search_terms: diabetic retinopathy aging mechanisms oxidative stress

name: Skin Aging and Photoaging
description: Cumulative intrinsic and UV-driven skin aging manifesting as wrinkles, loss of elasticity, hyperpigmentation, and impaired wound healing. Loss of dermal fibroblast function, collagen degradation, and accumulation of senescent cells are primary mechanisms.

mechanism_links.notes: Senescent dermal fibroblasts secrete MMPs degrading ECM
Paradoxical collagen crosslinking with MMP-mediated degradation
UV-generated ROS damage dermal DNA, proteins, and lipids
Skin epigenetic clock closely tracks photoaging severity

name: Psoriasis (Late-Onset)
description: Chronic immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease with keratinocyte hyperproliferation and T cell-driven skin inflammation. Late-onset psoriasis (>40 years) shows distinct immunological features related to immunosenescence and inflammaging.

mechanism_links.notes: NF-κB drives keratinocyte hyperproliferation and cytokine production
Age-related shift in Th17/Treg balance promotes psoriatic inflammation
Senescent keratinocytes contribute to psoriatic skin inflammation
Gut dysbiosis and intestinal permeability correlate with psoriasis severity

name: Impaired Wound Healing
description: Age-related decline in wound healing capacity manifesting as chronic non-healing ulcers. Reduced keratinocyte and fibroblast proliferative capacity, impaired immune cell recruitment, senescent cells impairing regeneration, and poor vascularization all contribute.

mechanism_links.notes: Epidermal stem cell niche dysfunction impairs re-epithelialization
Senescent fibroblasts fail to contract wounds and overexpress MMPs
Microvascular aging impairs angiogenic response to injury
Impaired macrophage phase transitions delay wound resolution

name: Immunosenescence
description: Age-related decline and dysregulation of the immune system characterized by thymic involution, T cell repertoire contraction, impaired vaccine responses, and paradoxical chronic inflammation. Drives susceptibility to infections, cancer, and autoimmunity.

mechanism_links.notes: Telomere shortening in lymphocytes drives replicative senescence
Thymic niche degeneration reduces naive T cell output
Epigenetic aging of immune cells tracks immune functional decline

name: Autoimmune Thyroiditis (Hashimoto's)
description: Chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis driven by autoreactive T and B cells destroying thyroid follicular cells. Prevalence increases with age; immunosenescence-associated breakdown of self-tolerance underlies late-onset presentation.

mechanism_links.notes: Regulatory T cell decline allows autoreactive lymphocyte expansion
NF-κB drives local thyroid inflammation and cytokine production
Epigenetic drift in regulatory T cells impairs self-tolerance
Intestinal dysbiosis and permeability correlate with thyroid autoimmunity

name: Late-Onset Autoimmune Diabetes (LADA)
description: Slowly progressive autoimmune diabetes in adults, sharing features of type 1 and type 2 diabetes. Immunosenescence may paradoxically permit slow immune-mediated beta-cell destruction while metabolic stress and aging accelerate progression.

mechanism_links.notes: Altered regulatory T cell function permits chronic beta-cell autoimmunity
Beta-cell ER stress under autoimmune attack and metabolic load
Peripheral insulin resistance accelerates beta-cell exhaustion
Islet inflammation via NF-κB in infiltrating T cells and macrophages

name: Late-Onset Depression
description: Major depressive disorder with onset after age 60, associated with vascular changes, neuroinflammation, hippocampal volume loss, and HPA axis dysregulation. Distinct from early-onset depression in its biological substrate and poor treatment response.

mechanism_links.notes: Elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines directly induce depressive symptoms
White matter hyperintensities from small vessel disease common in late-life depression
NAD+/SIRT1 pathway regulates BDNF and synaptic plasticity
Prefrontal and hippocampal synaptic loss contributes to depressive phenotype

name: Late-Onset Anxiety
description: Generalized anxiety disorder and anxiety syndromes arising or persisting in older age. Driven by neurobiological changes including amygdala hyperreactivity, prefrontal decline, HPA axis hyperactivation, and neuroinflammation interacting with psychosocial stressors.

mechanism_links.notes: IL-1β and TNF-α alter limbic circuit function and anxiety behavior
Prefrontal GABAergic circuit decline reduces anxiety inhibition
HPA axis dysregulation and cortisol elevation promote anxiety
Oxidative stress in amygdala promotes hyperreactive fear circuits

name: Dementia-Related Psychosis
description: Psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations) occurring in the context of neurodegenerative dementia, particularly Lewy body dementia, Parkinson's dementia, and Alzheimer's disease. Dopaminergic and cholinergic imbalance, neuroinflammation, and cortical Lewy body burden are key contributors.

mechanism_links.notes: Cortical Lewy bodies disrupt visual and association cortex function
Microglial activation correlates with psychotic symptoms in dementia
Cholinergic and dopaminergic synaptic loss drives psychotic phenotype
Protein aggregates disrupt neurotransmitter signaling circuits

name: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
description: Chronic relapsing inflammatory disorders of the gastrointestinal tract including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. Late-onset IBD (>60 years) is increasingly recognized; aging-associated dysbiosis, intestinal barrier impairment, and immunosenescence contribute to a distinct pathobiology.

mechanism_links.notes: Barrier dysfunction allows luminal bacteria to trigger mucosal inflammation
Reduced butyrate impairs colonocyte energy and barrier function
NF-κB constitutively active in inflamed intestinal epithelium
Dysregulated immune tolerance in aged intestinal mucosa

name: Colorectal Polyps
description: Premalignant adenomatous polyps of the colorectum arising from accumulated somatic mutations and epigenetic alterations in colonocytes. Incidence rises sharply with age; serrated and advanced adenomas carry highest cancer risk.

mechanism_links.notes: CpG island methylator phenotype (CIMP) characterizes serrated polyps
Bacterial genotoxins accumulate mutation burden in colonocytes
Senescent colonic stromal cells create polyp-promoting microenvironment
Hyperinsulinemia promotes colonocyte proliferation via IGF-1R

name: Hepatic Fibrosis
description: Excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix proteins in the liver resulting from chronic injury or NASH, leading to cirrhosis and liver failure. Hepatic stellate cell activation by TGF-β, oxidative stress, and SASP factors drives progressive fibrosis.

mechanism_links.notes: TGF-β-mediated hepatic stellate cell activation and collagen deposition
Senescent hepatocytes secrete TGF-β and inflammatory SASP factors
Portal LPS activates hepatic TLR4 driving stellate cell activation
Mitochondrial ROS in hepatocytes activate JNK and TGF-β pathways

name: Barrett's Esophagus
description: Metaplastic replacement of esophageal squamous epithelium by intestinal-type columnar epithelium in response to chronic acid reflux. Age-related reduction in lower esophageal sphincter tone and increased prevalence of GERD drive Barrett's metaplasia and adenocarcinoma risk.

Senescent esophageal epithelial cells create inflammatory niche
Acid and bile-induced ROS damage esophageal epithelial DNA
Acid reflux activates NF-κB promoting intestinal metaplasia genes

name: Menopause-Related Conditions

Estrogen loss removes osteoclast suppression causing rapid bone loss
Loss of estrogen's vasculo-protective effects accelerates atherosclerosis
Menopause-associated visceral adiposity shift increases metabolic risk

name: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia
description: Non-malignant enlargement of the prostate gland causing lower urinary tract symptoms in aging men. Driven by age-related hormonal changes (DHT/estrogen ratio), prostatic stromal senescence, and inflammation creating a proliferative microenvironment.

Senescent prostatic stromal cells secrete growth factors stimulating epithelial proliferation
Chronic prostatic inflammation via NF-κB promotes stromal growth
Hyperinsulinemia and IGF-1 elevation promote prostatic cell proliferation

name: Erectile Dysfunction

Testosterone decline reduces libido and smooth muscle responsiveness
Superoxide scavenges NO in penile vasculature, impairing erection
Senescent cavernous endothelial cells reduce vascular responsiveness

description: Unexplained normocytic anemia in older adults not attributable to nutritional deficiency, renal disease, or bone marrow failure. Driven by impaired erythropoiesis, chronic low-grade inflammation, and declining hematopoietic stem cell function with age.

Bone marrow niche aging impairs erythroid progenitor expansion
CHIP clones can outcompete normal erythroid progenitors
Mitochondrial dysfunction impairs erythroid differentiation energy demand

name: Thrombocytopenia of Aging

Oxidative damage to megakaryocyte progenitors impairs thrombopoiesis

name: Adrenal Insufficiency
description: Deficient glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid production from adrenal cortex atrophy or autoimmune destruction. Aging-related adrenal atrophy reduces reserve capacity; DHEA and cortisol dynamics are altered with age. Autoimmune Addison's disease prevalence increases with immunosenescence.

mechanism_links.notes: Adrenal hormone axis impairment is the primary mechanism
Autoimmune adrenalitis driven by regulatory T cell dysfunction
ROS damage adrenocortical mitochondria-rich steroidogenic cells
Mitochondria are critical for steroidogenesis in adrenal cortex cells

name: Growth Hormone Deficiency (Somatopause)
description: Age-related decline in growth hormone secretion and IGF-1 levels (somatopause) contributing to sarcopenia, adiposity, impaired immune function, and frailty. GH pulse amplitude and frequency decline progressively after age 30.

mechanism_links.notes: Somatotroph axis decline with aging is the defining mechanism
GH/IGF-1 axis is a major anabolic signal for skeletal muscle
GH/IGF-1 promotes mitochondrial biogenesis via PGC-1α
